topic_summary: Every variable in Java has a fixed, declared type — a number, some text, a true/false — and Java enforces that type the moment you try to use the variable, not later.
learning_objective: Declare variables using Java's primitive types and understand the difference between primitives and objects.
common_misconceptions: Assuming Java infers a variable's type from its value, the way a dynamically typed language would, instead of requiring an explicit declared type.
Assuming a type mismatch (e.g. assigning text to an int) is caught at runtime, when Java actually catches it at compile time.
Not realizing whole-number arithmetic can silently overflow rather than error, producing a wrong result instead of a crash.
core_concepts: Java is statically typed: you declare a variable's type up front, and Java holds you to it.
The 8 primitive types cover the basic building blocks (int, double, boolean, char, and others).
A variable is a named, typed storage location — not just a label you can repoint to any kind of value.
Objects (like String) are different from primitives; this reel only previews that distinction.
simple_analogy: A variable is like a labeled storage bin at a warehouse: the label says the bin can only hold books, and the warehouse simply won't let you shelve a couch there — the type is locked in the moment the bin is created.
code_example_ideas: Declare an int, a double, and a boolean side by side, printing each with its type.
A one-line compiler error caused by assigning a String literal to an int variable.
int overflow demonstrated by adding 1 to Integer.MAX_VALUE.
beginner_explanation: In Java, every variable has to say what kind of value it will hold before you can use it — a number, some text, a true or false, and so on. Once you've picked a type, Java holds you to it: try to put the wrong kind of value in and the code won't even compile. That might feel strict, but it's what lets Java catch a whole category of bugs before your program ever runs.
advanced_notes: Primitive types are stored directly wherever the variable lives (the stack, for locals); reference types like String store a reference to heap-allocated data. That distinction belongs to Memory Management (JVM and Performance level) — don't front-load it here.
int overflow wraps around silently (two's complement) rather than throwing; Java does not check for overflow on primitive arithmetic by default.
safety_notes: Do not say Java 'infers' the type of a normal variable declaration — that's only true for var (local variable type inference), a separate, later concept. Saying it here would misrepresent how int x = 5; actually works.
Do not say int overflow throws an exception — it silently wraps around. Stating otherwise is a factual error any Java developer would catch immediately.

flagged_uncertainties: Whether to tease var (local variable type inference) at all was considered and deliberately left out rather than risk confusing viewers this early; flagged in case a future revision wants a short forward-reference.
